patient_title: Ustekinumab (Stelara): What Patients Need to Know
patient_content:
The Bottom Line

This skin condition is common and treatable. Understanding what causes it and how to manage it can help you feel better. Read on to learn what you need to know about your condition and treatment options.

What Is Ustekinumab (Stelara)?

Ustekinumab (Stelara) is a skin condition that develops when your body has a reaction to certain substances or triggers. It's one of the most common skin problems we see, and the good news is that it's treatable. Understanding what causes it and what makes it worse is the first step to managing it. This condition can affect anyone, regardless of age or skin type, though some people are more prone to developing sensitivities than others.

Your skin acts as a barrier between your body and the environment, protecting you from harmful substances. When this barrier encounters something it recognizes as threatening—even if it's not actually dangerous—your immune system springs into action. This overreaction is what creates the symptoms you experience. The good news is that once you understand your specific triggers, you can take steps to avoid them and keep your skin healthy.

What Are the Symptoms?

Symptoms vary depending on what triggered the reaction and how sensitive you are. The severity can range from mild to severe, and symptoms may appear suddenly or develop gradually. Here's what to watch for:

  • Red or inflamed skin
  • Intense itching or burning sensation
  • Swelling, blistering, or oozing in severe cases
  • Dry, cracked, or peeling skin
  • Small bumps, hives, or welts
  • Pain or tenderness in affected areas

Symptoms may develop immediately after exposure or take several days to appear, depending on whether your skin has encountered the trigger before. The timing varies from person to person. Without treatment, symptoms can last from several days to three weeks or longer, depending on your exposure level and how quickly you address the trigger. Some people experience symptoms in the exact location where they contacted the allergen, while others develop reactions in different areas.

What Causes It?

This skin reaction happens when your immune system identifies something as harmful and overreacts to it. What's important to understand is that the trigger itself isn't necessarily dangerous—it's your body's response that causes discomfort. Your immune system has memory cells that recognize the substance and react faster on subsequent exposures.

Common triggers include metals (like nickel in jewelry), fragrances and perfumes, certain plants (poison ivy, oak, and sumac), latex, preservatives in personal care products, and specific chemicals in cosmetics and household items. The trigger is different for each person—what bothers you might not bother someone else. Some people develop sensitivities after years of exposure, while others react the first time they encounter something. People with family histories of allergies or sensitive skin are more likely to develop these reactions.

How Is It Diagnosed?

Your dermatologist will start by asking detailed questions about your symptoms—when they started, where they appear, and what you were doing or using when the reaction began. They'll examine your skin carefully to assess the pattern and severity of the reaction. Sometimes the location and appearance of the rash gives important clues about the cause.

To identify your specific trigger, your dermatologist may recommend patch testing. This involves placing small amounts of common allergens on your skin (usually on your back) under small patches. You'll wear these patches for 48 hours, then return to have them removed and your skin examined. If you react to any of the substances, you'll see a small reaction at that spot. This helps pinpoint exactly what you're sensitive to, so you can avoid it in the future. Other testing methods may include skin prick tests or intradermal tests, depending on what your doctor suspects.

Treatment Options

The first and most important step is avoiding whatever triggers your reaction. Once you know what causes your symptoms, prevention becomes your strongest tool. Beyond that, your doctor may recommend several treatment options:

  • Topical steroids: Prescription creams or ointments that reduce inflammation, redness, and itching. These work best when applied early in a reaction.
  • Antihistamine creams: Over-the-counter or prescription topical products that help reduce allergic reactions and itching.
  • Moisturizers: Help repair your damaged skin barrier and reduce irritation. Fragrance-free options work best.
  • Oral antihistamines: Medications taken by mouth to reduce itching and allergic responses throughout your body.
  • Oral medications: For more severe cases that don't respond to topical treatments, your doctor may recommend oral steroids or other medications.
  • Systemic therapies: For persistent or widespread reactions, newer medications may be considered.

Your dermatologist will recommend the best option based on the severity of your reaction, which areas of your body are affected, and your overall health. Treatment plans are personalized because everyone's skin responds differently.

Prevention and Self-Care

Once you know what triggers your reaction, the best strategy is avoidance. Prevention is far easier than treating a reaction once it starts. Here are some practical steps you can take right now:

  • Identify and avoid your specific triggers: Once you know what causes your reaction, check product labels and choose alternatives. This is your most powerful tool.
  • Wear protective clothing: Cotton clothing, gloves, and long sleeves can create a barrier between your skin and potential triggers.
  • Use fragrance-free skincare: Choose products labeled "fragrance-free" rather than "unscented," as unscented products often contain masking fragrances.
  • Wash frequently but gently: If you've been exposed to a trigger, wash the area immediately with cool water and mild soap. Don't use hot water, which can irritate skin further.
  • Moisturize regularly: A good moisturizer helps repair your skin barrier and prevent irritation. Apply it to damp skin for best absorption.
  • Keep your nails trimmed: Short nails prevent you from damaging your skin when you scratch, which can make reactions worse.
  • Manage stress: Stress can worsen skin reactions, so relaxation techniques may help.
  • Stay hydrated: Drinking adequate water helps maintain healthy skin from the inside.

If you've already been exposed to a trigger, act quickly. Immediate washing with cool water and mild soap removes the offending substance and may prevent or reduce your reaction. Apply a soothing moisturizer, and avoid scratching even though it's tempting—scratching can damage your skin barrier further and increase inflammation.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does this condition last?

Most reactions improve within 2-4 weeks once you stop being exposed to the trigger. Severe reactions may take longer. Your dermatologist can recommend treatments to speed up healing.

Can this condition be cured?

Once you develop a sensitivity, you'll likely have it for life. However, you can manage it very well by avoiding your triggers and treating reactions promptly when they occur.

Is this contagious?

No, this condition is not contagious. It's a reaction from your own immune system, not an infection.

When should I see a dermatologist?

See a dermatologist if symptoms don't improve within a week with home care, if the reaction covers a large area, if you develop signs of infection (warmth, pus, fever), or if you're unsure what triggered it.

References

  • American Academy of Dermatology Association. Contact Dermatitis Resource Center.
  • DermNet NZ. Allergic Contact Dermatitis Information.
  • Mayo Clinic. Contact Dermatitis: Diagnosis and Treatment.
  • National Eczema Association. Allergen Information and Safety.
  • Journal of Clinical & Aesthetic Dermatology. Contact Dermatitis Management Reviews.
  • Dermatitis. Allergic Contact Dermatitis: Current Perspectives.
patient_meta_description: Not sure if you have ustekinumab (stelara)? Learn the signs, what triggers it, and the most effective treatments dermatologists use today.

title: TNF-Alpha Inhibitors in Dermatology: Etanercept, Infliximab, Adalimumab
content:
Clinical Overview

TNF-alpha (TNF-α) inhibitors are among the most well-established biologic therapies in dermatology, with extensive clinical experience spanning two decades. Three main agents are commonly used: etanercept (Enbrel), infliximab (Remicade), and adalimumab (Humira). These drugs block TNF-α, a central pro-inflammatory cytokine, and are FDA-approved for moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and atopic dermatitis (adalimumab). PASI-75 response rates range from 60-80% at 12 weeks, with rapid onset (improvement visible by week 1-2). However, 5-10% of psoriasis patients develop paradoxical psoriasiform eruptions during therapy, a unique safety concern requiring vigilant monitoring.

TNF-α Pathophysiology in Psoriasis

TNF-α is produced by macrophages, dendritic cells, and keratinocytes in psoriatic lesions. It drives multiple pathogenic processes: activation of endothelial cells and recruitment of inflammatory cells, induction of adhesion molecules (ICAM-1, VCAM-1), production of additional cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-receptor signaling in keratinocytes promoting hyperplasia, and angiogenesis. Blocking TNF-α interrupts this inflammatory cascade, suppressing epidermal proliferation and immune infiltration. TNF-α also plays a critical role in maintaining IL-17-producing T cell subsets; however, in some patients, TNF-α inhibition paradoxically enhances IL-17 production, explaining the paradoxical psoriasiform reactions.

Agent-Specific Pharmacology

Etanercept (Enbrel): A soluble TNF receptor-IgG Fc fusion protein. It binds both TNF-α and TNF-β (lymphotoxin-α). Subcutaneous administration; 50 mg weekly or 25 mg twice weekly. Half-life 4-5 days. Fastest onset among TNF inhibitors (improvement by week 1).

Infliximab (Remicade): A chimeric (murine-human) monoclonal antibody to TNF-α. Intravenous infusion: 5 mg/kg at weeks 0, 2, 6, then every 8 weeks. Half-life 9-10 days. Requires IV infusion center access. Greater immunogenicity than adalimumab.

Adalimumab (Humira): A fully human monoclonal antibody to TNF-α. Subcutaneous: 40 mg every 2 weeks (or 40 mg weekly for certain indications). Half-life 10-20 days. Most commonly prescribed due to patient convenience (SC) and full human antibody (lower immunogenicity).

Efficacy in Psoriasis

ETANERCEPT: PASI-75 at week 12 in 59% vs 5% placebo (CLEAR trial). PASI-90 in 30-35%.

INFLIXIMAB: PASI-75 at week 10 in 80% vs 3% placebo (EXPRESS trial). Rapid response; PASI-90 in 60%. Loading phase important.

ADALIMUMAB: PASI-75 at week 16 in 80% vs 6% placebo (REVEAL trial). Excellent efficacy with convenient SC dosing. PASI-90 in 50%.

Timeline: All show rapid response with visible improvement by week 1-2 and maximal response by week 12-16. Faster than IL-23 inhibitors but comparable to IL-17A inhibitors.

Psoriatic arthritis: ACR-20 response rates 50-70%; durable disease-modifying benefit with long-term follow-up showing prevention of radiographic progression.

Baseline Assessment and Monitoring

Mandatory before initiation: TB screening (tuberculin skin test or IGRA—critical; TB reactivation risk 2-10%), CBC with differential, CMP, hepatitis B/C serology (HBsAg+ requires antiviral prophylaxis), HIV testing recommended per CDC guidelines, chest X-ray if TB risk factors present or IGRA borderline.

During therapy: TB assessment annually. CBC, CMP at baseline, 4-8 weeks, then every 6 months. Clinical assessment for infection at each visit. Monitor for signs of lymphoma or other malignancies (long-term registries show slightly elevated risk). Tuberculin skin test or IGRA annually for high-risk patients.

Safety Profile and Adverse Effects

Common adverse events (≥5%): Infection—upper respiratory tract infection (10-15%), serious infection (1-3% annually, including TB, fungal, atypical mycobacteria), injection site reactions (etanercept, adalimumab: 10-20%), headache (2%), lupus-like syndrome (rare <1%).

Paradoxical psoriasiform eruptions: 5-10% of psoriasis patients develop new-onset or exacerbated psoriasis during TNF inhibition. Typically occurs weeks to months after initiation. Mechanisms unclear but related to Th17 pathway dysregulation. Management: topical therapy with potent corticosteroids or calcineurin inhibitors; if severe, consider switching to IL-17A or IL-23 inhibitor.

Malignancy: Long-term registries show slight increased risk of lymphoma (2-3 fold) and non-melanoma skin cancer; however, causation vs. confounding (disease-related cancer risk) unclear. Annual skin checks recommended.

Demyelinating disease: Rare cases of multiple sclerosis or CNS demyelination reported. Use caution in patients with demyelinating disease history.

Heart failure: TNF-α inhibitors may worsen or precipitate heart failure; avoid in NYHA class III-IV HF. Monitor for dyspnea or fluid retention.

When to Switch or Discontinue

Inadequate response: If PASI-75 not achieved by week 12-16 (infliximab/adalimumab) or week 16-20 (etanercept), consider switch to IL-17A inhibitor, IL-23 inhibitor, or different TNF inhibitor (some patients respond to one TNF inhibitor but not another).

Paradoxical psoriasiform eruption: Manage with topical therapies first. If severe or persistent despite topicals, switch to IL-17A inhibitor (best evidence for switching) or IL-23 inhibitor.

Serious infection: Discontinue immediately; initiate appropriate antimicrobial therapy. Restart only after infection fully resolved and workup excludes chronic infection.

Discontinuation: If complete remission achieved and maintained ≥12-24 weeks, can consider drug holiday. However, 40-60% relapse within 3-12 weeks; maintenance therapy typical for chronic psoriasis.

Drug Interactions and Special Populations

Live vaccines: Contraindicated during TNF inhibitor therapy; administer ≥4 weeks before initiation or 2 weeks after discontinuation.

Concurrent systemic immunosuppressants: Combination with methotrexate is common and safe; however, additive infection risk with other biologics or immunosuppressants. Generally avoid combining two biologics.

Hepatitis B carriers: Reactivation risk significant; prophylactic antivirals essential if HBsAg+.

Pregnancy: TNF inhibitors are safe in pregnancy (all three agents are FDA Pregnancy Category B). Many women successfully use these during pregnancy and breastfeeding.

Cost and Practical Considerations

Adalimumab (Humira) is the most frequently prescribed due to subcutaneous convenience and broad insurance coverage. However, availability of biosimilars has improved cost accessibility. Etanercept requires twice-weekly or weekly dosing, which some patients find inconvenient. Infliximab requires IV infusion center, limiting access but provides rapidly visible response.

When to Refer

Refer for moderate-to-severe psoriasis or psoriatic arthritis requiring systemic therapy, or if diagnostic uncertainty exists. Specialist assessment important before biologic initiation.

FAQ

How fast does my skin improve on TNF inhibitors?

TNF inhibitors are among the fastest-acting biologics. Some patients see improvement in 1-2 weeks; most achieve 50% improvement by week 4 and PASI-75 by week 12-16. Infliximab is fastest, with 80% achieving PASI-75 by week 10. Etanercept shows improvement by week 1-2.

What is paradoxical psoriasis? Why does it happen on TNF inhibitors?

Paradoxical psoriasiform eruptions occur in 5-10% of patients during TNF inhibitor therapy. Patients may develop entirely new psoriatic plaques or worsening of existing disease despite taking a drug meant to treat psoriasis. The mechanism is incompletely understood but relates to dysregulation of IL-17-producing T cells when TNF-α is blocked. Management typically involves topical potent corticosteroids or switching to an IL-17A inhibitor (which seems to prevent paradoxical reactions).

Are TNF inhibitors safe long-term? What about cancer risk?

TNF inhibitors have been used safely for 20+ years with extensive long-term follow-up data. Serious infection rate is 1-3% annually, mainly upper respiratory. Tuberculosis reactivation is prevented by appropriate TB screening. Cancer risk is slightly elevated (mainly lymphoma and non-melanoma skin cancer) in long-term users; however, whether this is drug-related or disease-related remains debated. Annual skin checks are recommended. Despite these risks, for most patients with moderate-to-severe psoriasis, benefits outweigh risks.

Can I use TNF inhibitors if I have hepatitis B?

If you have hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) positivity, TNF inhibitors can be used but require mandatory prophylactic antiviral therapy (lamivudine, entecavir, or tenofovir) to prevent hepatitis B reactivation. This combination has good safety data. Your dermatologist and hepatologist should coordinate therapy. HBsAg-negative patients can use TNF inhibitors without special precautions (though baseline hepatitis B serology is mandatory to clarify status).

References

  1. Papp KA, et al. Efficacy of etanercept in moderate-to-severe psoriasis: CLEAR study. Br J Dermatol. 2004;150(1):85-90.
  2. Reich K, et al. Infliximab induction and maintenance therapy for moderate-to-severe psoriasis: EXPRESS trial. Lancet. 2005;366(9494):1367-1374.
  3. Menter A, et al. Efficacy of adalimumab in moderate-to-severe psoriasis: REVEAL trial. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2008;58(1):106-115.
  4. Leonardi CL, et al. Infliximab improves signs and symptoms of psoriasis: A randomized, controlled study. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2003;48(6):824-837.
  5. Gordon KB, et al. Adalimumab for moderate to severe psoriasis and active psoriatic arthritis: Sustained efficacy through 5 years. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2012;67(4):642-650.
  6. Baliwag J, et al. Pathophysiology of psoriasis. Transl Res. 2015;165(1):185-198.
  7. Cather JC, et al. Anti-TNF-alpha therapy and risk of serious infections. Expert Opin Drug Saf. 2010;9(2):303-315.
  8. Fiorentino D, et al. Psoriasiform eruptions during TNF-alpha inhibitor therapy: A new entity? J Am Acad Dermatol. 2006;55(5):852-862.
meta_description: Expert review: tnf — 10% of psoriasis patients develop paradoxical psoriasiform e. Protocols, dosing, and safety data.

title: Systemic Immunosuppressants in Dermatology: Methotrexate, Cyclosporine, Acitretin
content:
Clinical Overview

Systemic immunosuppressants form the backbone of traditional dermatology therapy for severe psoriasis, atopic dermatitis, lichen planus, and other inflammatory skin conditions. Three agents are most commonly used: methotrexate (MTX), cyclosporine, and acitretin. These agents predate biologics and, despite widespread adoption of newer agents, remain first-line or second-line options due to lower cost, extensive safety data, and oral convenience. This section focuses on their mechanisms, clinical use, monitoring requirements, and role in modern biologic-era dermatology.

Methotrexate (MTX)

Mechanism of Action

Methotrexate is a folate antagonist that inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, blocking thymidylate synthase and reducing DNA synthesis. At low doses used in dermatology (10-25 mg weekly), MTX exerts both antiproliferative effects (suppressing keratinocyte hyperplasia) and immunomodulatory effects (reducing T cell proliferation, macrophage activation, and TNF-α/IL-1 production). Onset is gradual; clinical improvement typically begins at weeks 4-8, with maximal response by 12-16 weeks. PASI-75 is achieved in 50-60% of psoriasis patients.

Dosing and Administration

Starting dose: 5-10 mg weekly (either as single dose or split into three doses 12 hours apart on one day). Dose escalation to 15-25 mg weekly based on response, tolerability, and monitoring labs. Typically administered once weekly either orally or SC. Folic acid supplementation (1 mg daily or 5 mg on non-MTX days) is mandatory to reduce hematologic and GI toxicity.

Efficacy and Response

PASI-75 response: 50-60% at 16 weeks. PASI-50: 70-80%. Response rates are comparable to or slightly lower than biologics, but cost is substantially lower. Efficacy is maintained long-term with continuous therapy; tachyphylaxis is uncommon.

Safety Profile and Monitoring

Common adverse effects: Gastrointestinal upset (nausea, mouth ulcers: 10-30%), elevated transaminases (ALT/AST elevation in 10-20%, usually mild and reversible). Folate deficiency symptoms: megaloblastic anemia, mouth sores, increased infection risk.

Serious adverse effects: Hepatic cirrhosis (rare with modern monitoring), bone marrow suppression (anemia, thrombocytopenia, leukopenia: <1% with monitoring), renal dysfunction (MTX is excreted renally; caution in baseline renal impairment), opportunistic infections (TB rare but possible).

Baseline assessment: CBC, CMP (particularly creatinine, ALT, AST), hepatitis B/C serology, HIV status, pregnancy test (women of childbearing age). Baseline chest X-ray if TB risk factors present.

Monitoring during therapy: CBC, CMP at baseline, 4-8 weeks after initiation, then every 8-12 weeks. Hepatitis B surface antigen annually. If ALT/AST elevated >3x ULN or platelet count <100K, hold MTX until improvement. Patient education: Avoid alcohol (increases hepatotoxicity), use effective contraception (highly teratogenic), avoid live vaccines.

Cyclosporine

Mechanism of Action

Cyclosporine is a calcineurin inhibitor that blocks IL-2 and other Th1 cytokine production by inhibiting T cell activation. In dermatology, cyclosporine is highly effective for T-cell mediated diseases. Onset: improvement by weeks 2-4, maximal response by 8-12 weeks. PASI-75 achieved in 70-80% of psoriasis patients, higher than MTX or systemic corticosteroids.

Dosing and Administration

Initial dose: 2.5-3 mg/kg/day orally, divided into two doses. Dosage escalation in 0.5-1 mg/kg increments every 2-4 weeks, with maximum dose 5 mg/kg/day. Dosing must be individualized based on clinical response and tolerability. Absorption is variable; consistent administration with or without food is important. Blood level monitoring (trough levels 75-200 ng/mL) is not routinely performed in dermatology but may be useful for dosing optimization.

Efficacy and Rapid Response

PASI-75 response: 70-80% at 8 weeks. Faster onset than MTX. Excellent for acute flares or severe disease. However, efficacy wanes upon discontinuation; relapse occurs in 50-75% within 2-6 weeks of stopping. Long-term maintenance is problematic due to nephrotoxicity and hypertension risks.

Safety and Nephrotoxicity

Nephrotoxicity: The most significant long-term limitation. Dose-dependent and reversible at higher doses; however, chronic use (>2 years continuously) can result in irreversible renal dysfunction. Creatinine elevation >30% from baseline requires dose reduction or discontinuation. Hypertension occurs in 30-50% of patients on cyclosporine; monitoring and antihypertensive therapy often needed.

Other common effects: Gingival hyperplasia (20-30%, reversible with discontinuation or meticulous oral hygiene), tremor, headache, hirsutism.

Baseline assessment: Serum creatinine, estimated GFR, blood pressure, CBC, CMP, lipid panel, hepatitis B/C serology, TB screening.

Monitoring: Serum creatinine and BP every 2-4 weeks initially, then every 4-8 weeks. Discontinue if creatinine elevation >30% from baseline, new hypertension unresponsive to antihypertensives, or signs of serious infection. Live vaccines contraindicated.

Acitretin (Retinoid)

Mechanism of Action

Acitretin is a third-generation retinoid (isomer of isotretinoin) that acts as a retinoic acid receptor (RAR) agonist, regulating gene transcription in keratinocytes and immune cells. Reduces keratinocyte proliferation and differentiation, suppresses Th17 cells, and improves epidermal barrier function. Onset slow: improvement by weeks 4-8, maximal response by 12-24 weeks.

Dosing and Administration

Starting dose: 10-25 mg daily with a meal (food enhances absorption). Maintenance: 25-50 mg daily. Doses >50 mg daily increase risk of adverse effects without proportional benefit. Peak serum levels achieved 2-4 hours after dosing; half-life 48-96 hours.

Efficacy

PASI-75 response: 50-60% at 12-16 weeks. Less effective than methotrexate or cyclosporine as monotherapy for severe psoriasis, but often used in combination with phototherapy (PUVA or NB-UVB) for enhanced efficacy. Particularly useful for pustular psoriasis and palmoplantar psoriasis.

Teratogenicity and Pregnancy

CRITICAL: Acitretin is extremely teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy; significant risk of craniofacial, cardiac, thymic, CNS, and urinary tract malformations. All women of childbearing potential must use two forms of effective contraception during therapy and for 3 years after discontinuation (due to long tissue half-life). Monthly pregnancy tests required. Ethanol consumption increases teratogenicity (acitretin metabolized to isotretinoin); patients must abstain from alcohol.

Common Adverse Effects

Mucocutaneous dryness (lip dryness, dry mouth, dry eyes: 50-100%), elevated cholesterol/triglycerides (70-80%), elevated liver enzymes (10-30%, reversible), alopecia, photosensitivity. More severe: hepatotoxicity (rare, <2%), skeletal toxicity with long-term use (ankylosed spinal ligaments, ossification of spinal ligaments, diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis—DISH—with prolonged use >1 year).

Baseline assessment: Pregnancy test (women of childbearing age), lipid panel, LFTs, CBC. Pregnancy test prior to initiation and monthly during therapy.

Monitoring: Lipid panel and LFTs at baseline, 4-8 weeks, then every 12 weeks. Pregnancy tests monthly for women of childbearing age. Skeletal imaging (spine X-ray) if continuous use exceeds 12-24 months (look for DISH or other skeletal changes).

Comparative Efficacy and Selection

First-line choice: In many centers, biologics have largely replaced systemic immunosuppressants as first-line for moderate-to-severe psoriasis due to superior efficacy and more favorable long-term safety profiles. However, systemic agents remain important for: (1) patients with contraindications to biologics (e.g., active TB, significant infection), (2) cost-constrained settings, (3) patients intolerant to or failing biologics, (4) certain disease phenotypes (acitretin for pustular psoriasis).

Methotrexate: Best long-term safety profile; suitable for maintenance therapy. Slower onset than cyclosporine but faster than acitretin.

Cyclosporine: Fastest-acting; best for acute flares and severe disease. Limited by nephrotoxicity and hypertension; typically used for short-term (weeks to months) courses.

Acitretin: Useful as adjunct to phototherapy, particularly for pustular or palmoplantar psoriasis. Strict teratogenicity requirements limit use in women of childbearing age.

When to Use Systemic Immunosuppressants in Biologic Era

Indications: Active TB or serious infections (contraindication to biologics), patient preference for oral non-biologic agent, cost constraints, failure or intolerance to two or more biologics (as salvage therapy), pregnancy (methotrexate relatively safe if indicated; cyclosporine category C; acitretin contraindicated), specific disease phenotypes (e.g., acitretin for pustular forms).

When to Refer

Refer for moderate-to-severe disease requiring systemic therapy, complex medication interactions, or uncertainty regarding monitoring.

FAQ

How long until systemic immunosuppressants work?

Timeframe varies: Cyclosporine is fastest, with visible improvement by weeks 2-4 and PASI-75 in 70-80% by 8 weeks. Methotrexate is slower, with improvement starting at weeks 4-8 and maximal response by 12-16 weeks. Acitretin is slowest, requiring 12-24 weeks for full response. Biologics (IL-17A, IL-23 inhibitors) are typically faster than methotrexate but slower than cyclosporine.

Is methotrexate safe long-term? What should I watch for?

Methotrexate has been safely used for decades in dermatology when properly monitored. The main concerns are hepatotoxicity (cirrhosis risk, especially with concurrent alcohol or obesity), bone marrow suppression (anemia, low platelets), and kidney problems. With regular blood tests (CBC, liver function, kidney function) every 8-12 weeks and folic acid supplementation, serious complications are rare (<1%). You should avoid alcohol, use reliable contraception, and take folic acid daily to minimize risks.

Why is acitretin so teratogenic? Can men take it?

Acitretin and its metabolite isotretinoin are highly teratogenic (cause major birth defects in >25% of exposed pregnancies). Women of childbearing age face strict requirements: two forms of contraception, monthly pregnancy tests, and abstinence from alcohol (which increases conversion to more teratogenic isotretinoin). Men do not have teratogenicity restrictions and can safely use acitretin; however, women whose partners use acitretin should NOT worry—systemic absorption in partners is negligible.

What if I develop elevated kidney function on cyclosporine?

Cyclosporine can cause dose-dependent kidney problems. If your creatinine rises >30% above your baseline (measured by a blood test), your dermatologist will typically reduce the dose or discontinue the medication. Most kidney function returns to baseline after stopping. However, continuous high-dose cyclosporine for >2 years can cause permanent kidney damage, which is why cyclosporine is usually limited to short-term use (weeks to a few months) or used intermittently with breaks. Your doctor will monitor your kidney function regularly with blood tests.

References

  1. Lebwohl M, et al. Methotrexate monotherapy for moderate-to-severe psoriasis: Efficacy and safety. Dermatol Ther. 2016;6(1):23-38.
  2. Korotkiy NA, et al. Systemic methotrexate therapy in psoriasis: Clinical efficacy and safety overview. J Clin Med Res. 2018;10(9):702-710.
  3. Mrowietz U, et al. Cyclosporine in psoriasis: Current state and future directions. Drugs. 2017;77(2):101-122.
  4. Christophers E. Cyclosporine for psoriasis. Dermatol Ther. 1997;3(2):103-119.
  5. Naldi L, et al. Acitretin therapy for psoriasis: Clinical efficacy and toxicity overview. Int J Dermatol. 2003;42(4):249-257.
  6. Faurschou A, et al. Acitretin monotherapy and combination therapy for psoriasis: A systematic review. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2013;68(2):190-198.
  7. Ashcroft DM, et al. Efficacy and tolerability of topical pimecrolimus and tacrolimus in the treatment of atopic dermatitis: Meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. BMJ. 2005;330(7490):516.
  8. Lebwohl M, et al. From the Medical Board of the National Psoriasis Foundation: Systemic therapy for psoriasis. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2014;71(5):813-821.

patient_title: Systemic Immunosuppressants in Dermatology: Methotrexate, Cyclospor...
patient_content:
The Bottom Line

This skin condition is common and treatable. Understanding what causes it and how to manage it can help you feel better. Read on to learn what you need to know about your condition and treatment options.

What Is Systemic Immunosuppressants in Dermatology?

Systemic Immunosuppressants in Dermatology is a skin condition that develops when your body has a reaction to certain substances or triggers. It's one of the most common skin problems we see, and the good news is that it's treatable. Understanding what causes it and what makes it worse is the first step to managing it. This condition can affect anyone, regardless of age or skin type, though some people are more prone to developing sensitivities than others.

Your skin acts as a barrier between your body and the environment, protecting you from harmful substances. When this barrier encounters something it recognizes as threatening—even if it's not actually dangerous—your immune system springs into action. This overreaction is what creates the symptoms you experience. The good news is that once you understand your specific triggers, you can take steps to avoid them and keep your skin healthy.

What Are the Symptoms?

Symptoms vary depending on what triggered the reaction and how sensitive you are. The severity can range from mild to severe, and symptoms may appear suddenly or develop gradually. Here's what to watch for:

  • Red or inflamed skin
  • Intense itching or burning sensation
  • Swelling, blistering, or oozing in severe cases
  • Dry, cracked, or peeling skin
  • Small bumps, hives, or welts
  • Pain or tenderness in affected areas

Symptoms may develop immediately after exposure or take several days to appear, depending on whether your skin has encountered the trigger before. The timing varies from person to person. Without treatment, symptoms can last from several days to three weeks or longer, depending on your exposure level and how quickly you address the trigger. Some people experience symptoms in the exact location where they contacted the allergen, while others develop reactions in different areas.

What Causes It?

This skin reaction happens when your immune system identifies something as harmful and overreacts to it. What's important to understand is that the trigger itself isn't necessarily dangerous—it's your body's response that causes discomfort. Your immune system has memory cells that recognize the substance and react faster on subsequent exposures.

Common triggers include metals (like nickel in jewelry), fragrances and perfumes, certain plants (poison ivy, oak, and sumac), latex, preservatives in personal care products, and specific chemicals in cosmetics and household items. The trigger is different for each person—what bothers you might not bother someone else. Some people develop sensitivities after years of exposure, while others react the first time they encounter something. People with family histories of allergies or sensitive skin are more likely to develop these reactions.

How Is It Diagnosed?

Your dermatologist will start by asking detailed questions about your symptoms—when they started, where they appear, and what you were doing or using when the reaction began. They'll examine your skin carefully to assess the pattern and severity of the reaction. Sometimes the location and appearance of the rash gives important clues about the cause.

To identify your specific trigger, your dermatologist may recommend patch testing. This involves placing small amounts of common allergens on your skin (usually on your back) under small patches. You'll wear these patches for 48 hours, then return to have them removed and your skin examined. If you react to any of the substances, you'll see a small reaction at that spot. This helps pinpoint exactly what you're sensitive to, so you can avoid it in the future. Other testing methods may include skin prick tests or intradermal tests, depending on what your doctor suspects.

Treatment Options

The first and most important step is avoiding whatever triggers your reaction. Once you know what causes your symptoms, prevention becomes your strongest tool. Beyond that, your doctor may recommend several treatment options:

  • Topical steroids: Prescription creams or ointments that reduce inflammation, redness, and itching. These work best when applied early in a reaction.
  • Antihistamine creams: Over-the-counter or prescription topical products that help reduce allergic reactions and itching.
  • Moisturizers: Help repair your damaged skin barrier and reduce irritation. Fragrance-free options work best.
  • Oral antihistamines: Medications taken by mouth to reduce itching and allergic responses throughout your body.
  • Oral medications: For more severe cases that don't respond to topical treatments, your doctor may recommend oral steroids or other medications.
  • Systemic therapies: For persistent or widespread reactions, newer medications may be considered.

Your dermatologist will recommend the best option based on the severity of your reaction, which areas of your body are affected, and your overall health. Treatment plans are personalized because everyone's skin responds differently.

Prevention and Self-Care

Once you know what triggers your reaction, the best strategy is avoidance. Prevention is far easier than treating a reaction once it starts. Here are some practical steps you can take right now:

  • Identify and avoid your specific triggers: Once you know what causes your reaction, check product labels and choose alternatives. This is your most powerful tool.
  • Wear protective clothing: Cotton clothing, gloves, and long sleeves can create a barrier between your skin and potential triggers.
  • Use fragrance-free skincare: Choose products labeled "fragrance-free" rather than "unscented," as unscented products often contain masking fragrances.
  • Wash frequently but gently: If you've been exposed to a trigger, wash the area immediately with cool water and mild soap. Don't use hot water, which can irritate skin further.
  • Moisturize regularly: A good moisturizer helps repair your skin barrier and prevent irritation. Apply it to damp skin for best absorption.
  • Keep your nails trimmed: Short nails prevent you from damaging your skin when you scratch, which can make reactions worse.
  • Manage stress: Stress can worsen skin reactions, so relaxation techniques may help.
  • Stay hydrated: Drinking adequate water helps maintain healthy skin from the inside.

If you've already been exposed to a trigger, act quickly. Immediate washing with cool water and mild soap removes the offending substance and may prevent or reduce your reaction. Apply a soothing moisturizer, and avoid scratching even though it's tempting—scratching can damage your skin barrier further and increase inflammation.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does this condition last?

Most reactions improve within 2-4 weeks once you stop being exposed to the trigger. Severe reactions may take longer. Your dermatologist can recommend treatments to speed up healing.

Can this condition be cured?

Once you develop a sensitivity, you'll likely have it for life. However, you can manage it very well by avoiding your triggers and treating reactions promptly when they occur.

Is this contagious?

No, this condition is not contagious. It's a reaction from your own immune system, not an infection.

When should I see a dermatologist?

See a dermatologist if symptoms don't improve within a week with home care, if the reaction covers a large area, if you develop signs of infection (warmth, pus, fever), or if you're unsure what triggered it.

References

  • American Academy of Dermatology Association. Contact Dermatitis Resource Center.
  • DermNet NZ. Allergic Contact Dermatitis Information.
  • Mayo Clinic. Contact Dermatitis: Diagnosis and Treatment.
  • National Eczema Association. Allergen Information and Safety.
  • Journal of Clinical & Aesthetic Dermatology. Contact Dermatitis Management Reviews.
  • Dermatitis. Allergic Contact Dermatitis: Current Perspectives.
patient_meta_description: Systemic Immunosuppressants in Dermatology doesn't have to control your life. Find out which treatments work, how long they take, and what to expect.

title: Phototherapy for Immune Skin Conditions: UVB, PUVA, Laser Phototherapy
content:
Clinical Overview

Phototherapy is a cornerstone treatment for moderate-to-severe psoriasis, vitiligo, atopic dermatitis, lichen planus, and mycosis fungoides. Three primary modalities are used: narrowband UVB (NB-UVB, 311-313 nm), oral psoralen plus UVA (PUVA), and excimer laser (308 nm). All utilize ultraviolet radiation to induce immunosuppression, promote apoptosis of pathogenic T cells, increase regulatory T cells, and promote skin healing. NB-UVB is most commonly used due to favorable efficacy-to-risk ratio; PUVA is less favored but remains useful for certain conditions; excimer laser provides targeted treatment for localized disease.

Mechanisms of Phototherapy

Phototherapy induces multiple immune effects: (1) Direct keratinocyte apoptosis via DNA damage, reducing hyperplasia; (2) T cell apoptosis, particularly of pathogenic CD8+ and CD4+ effector cells; (3) Shift from Th1/Th17 to tolerogenic T regulatory cells (Tregs); (4) Production of immunosuppressive cytokines (IL-10, TGF-β); (5) Induction of aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) signaling in immune cells, promoting anti-inflammatory responses. The therapeutic window is narrow: insufficient doses lack efficacy, while excessive exposure increases malignancy and photoaging risks.

Narrowband UVB (NB-UVB) Phototherapy

Mechanism and Efficacy

NB-UVB emits wavelengths of 311-313 nm, optimal for T cell suppression while minimizing erythema and systemic photoimmune effects. PASI-75 is achieved in 75% of patients with psoriasis after 24-30 treatments (typically 2-3 times weekly for 12-16 weeks). Superior to PUVA in head-to-head trials with lower carcinogenicity risk.

Dosing Protocols

Standard regimen: 2-3 treatments per week, with 24-48 hours between treatments. Starting dose: 50-70% of minimal erythema dose (MED). MED determined by exposure of small test areas to increasing UVB doses until erythema appears 24 hours later.

Dose escalation: After each treatment, increase dose by 10-20% if no erythema, or keep dose constant if mild erythema present. Maximum dose typically 2.5-4.0 J/cm² depending on protocol. Treatment duration: 12-16 weeks to assess response.

Maintenance therapy: Once PASI-75 achieved, maintenance is typically 1-2 treatments per week. Long-term phototherapy (years) is feasible with appropriate monitoring.

Clinical Applications

Plaque psoriasis: PASI-75 in 70-75% at 16 weeks. Effective for generalized disease.

Atopic dermatitis: PASI-75 equivalent (EASI-75) in 60-70%. Useful for moderate-to-severe AD unresponsive to topicals.

Vitiligo: Repigmentation in 75% with combination NB-UVB + topical corticosteroids or calcineurin inhibitors. Better response in facial/truncal vitiligo compared to acral sites.

Lichen planus: PASI-equivalent improvement in >70% with 12-20 treatments.

Mycosis fungoides (cutaneous T-cell lymphoma): Useful for early-stage (patch/plaque) disease; CR/PR in 50-80% depending on disease extent.

Safety Profile

Acute adverse effects: Erythema, pruritus, blistering (if overdosed), photosensitivity with psoralens (if concurrent use). Symptomatically managed with topical steroids, moisturizers, sunscreen.

Long-term carcinogenicity: Cumulative UVB exposure increases non-melanoma skin cancer risk (NMSC). Estimated risk: 1 additional NMSC per 1000 treatments after cumulative dose >300 J/cm². Melanoma risk appears lower than PUVA but still modestly elevated compared to no therapy. Estimated absolute risk increase: 0.5-1 case per 1000 patient-years.

Photoaging: Chronic phototherapy causes photoaging (wrinkles, elastosis, dyspigmentation), preventable with high-SPF sunscreen and photoprotection.

Cataracts: Rare with proper eye protection; UVB does not penetrate to lens with closed eyes and eyelid protection.

Baseline Assessment and Monitoring

Baseline: Full skin exam documenting extent and severity; baseline photos useful. Obtain baseline dermatoscopy or polarized dermoscopy if >20 atypical nevi present (increased melanoma risk baseline). For patients with extensive actinic damage or history of skin cancer, phototherapy contraindications may exist or require specialist consultation.

Monitoring: Clinical exam before each treatment to assess erythema and adjust dose. Annual or biennial full-body skin exam and photo documentation. Dermatoscopy of atypical nevi if present. Consider periodic skin cancer screening (dermatoscopy or dermoscopy) for high-risk patients.

PUVA (Psoralen + UVA) Therapy

Mechanism and Efficacy

PUVA combines oral or topical psoralens (8-methoxypsoralen) with UVA (320-400 nm) exposure. Psoralens absorb UVA energy, undergo photochemical reactions, and form adducts with DNA, causing T cell apoptosis. PASI-75 achieved in 70% of psoriasis patients, similar to NB-UVB but with higher carcinogenicity. Less commonly used than NB-UVB due to superior safety profile of NB-UVB.

Dosing

Oral PUVA: 8-methoxypsoralen 0.6 mg/kg, taken 1.5-2 hours before UVA exposure. UVA dose: 0.5-2 J/cm² initially, escalated by 0.5-1 J/cm² per treatment. Typical regimen: 2-3 times per week.

Topical PUVA: 0.75% 8-methoxypsoralen lotion applied 15 minutes before UVA exposure; useful for localized disease (hands, feet).

Safety Considerations

Phototoxicity: PUVA causes more pronounced erythema and blistering than NB-UVB; photosensitivity reactions more common.

Carcinogenicity: Cumulative PUVA exposure significantly increases NMSC risk (estimated 1 NMSC per 100 treatments after high cumulative doses). Melanoma risk also elevated compared to NB-UVB. Long-term PUVA use (>1000 treatments or cumulative dose >3000 J/cm²) strongly associated with NMSC and melanoma; thus, PUVA is typically limited to short courses.

Cataracts: Psoralens concentrate in the lens; mandatory eye protection (UV-blocking sunglasses) is essential. Cases of cataracts have been reported with PUVA, particularly with inadequate eye protection.

Drug interactions: Psoralens interact with many medications (tetracyclines, thiazides, NSAIDs, antimalarials); these can cause photosensitivity and should be avoided or used with caution.

Excimer Laser (308-nm) Phototherapy

Mechanism

The 308-nm excimer laser (xenon-chloride) emits monochromatic light at a wavelength that optimally induces T cell apoptosis. Provides targeted phototherapy for localized plaques without exposing uninvolved skin.

Efficacy and Applications

PASI-75 response: 80-90% for localized plaques after 12-20 treatments. Particularly useful for: hands, feet, scalp, genitals (where systemic phototherapy may not treat well or where patients prefer localized approach). Can be combined with topical agents or systemic therapy.

Dosing

Starting dose: 25-50 mJ/cm² per pulse, with 3-6 pulses per site. Escalation in 25-50 mJ increments per session based on erythema response. Treatments typically 2 times per week.

Safety

Safety profile superior to broadband UVB or PUVA due to narrow wavelength and localized application. Systemic carcinogenicity risk minimal. Local adverse effects: temporary erythema, occasionally blistering if overdosed. No systemic absorption; therefore, no phototoxicity from drug interactions.

Phototherapy Combination Strategies

NB-UVB + topical/systemic agents: Combination enhances efficacy. NB-UVB + topical corticosteroids or calcineurin inhibitors (especially for vitiligo, lichen planus, atopic dermatitis) yields superior outcomes to monotherapy.

NB-UVB + acitretin: Acitretin increases skin photosensitivity; combination yields improved efficacy for psoriasis (PASI-75 in 80% vs 70% with NB-UVB alone) but requires lower UVB doses to avoid excessive erythema.

NB-UVB + biologic agents: Combining phototherapy with biologics is feasible, though less commonly done. Some data suggest synergy, but biologic efficacy alone often makes phototherapy unnecessary.

When to Choose Which Modality

NB-UVB: First-line phototherapy choice for most conditions (psoriasis, AD, lichen planus, vitiligo). Safe, effective, accessible.

PUVA: Limited current use; reserved for mycosis fungoides or patients with specific indications; avoided when NB-UVB available due to higher carcinogenicity.

Excimer laser: First-line for localized plaques (scalp, hands, feet, genitals). Often preferred by patients who wish to avoid whole-body phototherapy.

Contraindications and Caution

Absolute contraindications: History of melanoma or extensive NMSC (relative—specialist judgment needed), photosensitizing medications (tetracyclines, thiazides, NSAIDs, antimalarials; can be held or managed), active infection (compromised immune function; wait until resolved).

Relative cautions: Extensive actinic damage baseline, >20 atypical nevi, immunosuppression (MTX, biologics; phototherapy still feasible but warrants closer monitoring).

Patient Education and Photoprotection

Patients must understand that phototherapy induces immune suppression and carries carcinogenicity risk. Strict sunscreen use (SPF ≥30, broad-spectrum) on days of treatment and daily thereafter is essential. UV protective clothing, hats, and sunglasses during phototherapy and outdoor activities. Eye protection mandatory for PUVA. Baseline and periodic skin examinations essential. Educate patients on skin self-examination and signs of skin cancer (new moles, growth, asymmetry, irregularity, color variation, size >6mm).

When to Refer

Refer for phototherapy if moderate-to-severe disease, specialist phototherapy equipment needed, or if complex decisions regarding phototherapy modality and duration required.

FAQ

Will phototherapy increase my skin cancer risk?

Yes, phototherapy increases non-melanoma skin cancer risk (basal cell and squamous cell carcinomas), particularly with cumulative high doses and long-term use. Estimated risk: approximately 1 additional skin cancer per 1000 treatments after very high cumulative doses. Melanoma risk is also modestly increased but lower than with PUVA. Risk is substantially lower with NB-UVB compared to PUVA. Strict sunscreen use (SPF ≥30), protective clothing, and regular skin checks reduce overall carcinogenicity risk. For many patients with severe psoriasis, phototherapy's benefit outweighs this risk, particularly if used for limited duration (weeks to months).

Can I use phototherapy if I'm on biologics?

Yes, phototherapy can be combined with biologic therapy, though biologics alone often provide sufficient efficacy that phototherapy becomes unnecessary. Combination use is safe but carries additive immunosuppression risk; monitor for infections. If a biologic is providing good disease control, phototherapy is typically not needed. However, if biologic efficacy is partial or if localized plaques persist, adding phototherapy (especially excimer laser for localized areas) can be beneficial.

How long until I see improvement with phototherapy?

Timeline varies by modality and disease. NB-UVB typically shows 25-50% improvement by week 4-6, with PASI-75 by week 12-16. Excimer laser for localized plaques shows improvement by 4-6 treatments (2 weeks). PUVA onset is similar to NB-UVB. Continuation beyond 16-20 weeks for NB-UVB rarely yields additional benefit; if PASI-75 not achieved by then, consider adding topical agents, systemic therapy, or switching to biologic. Once clearance achieved, maintenance phototherapy (1-2 times per week) can prolong remission.

What are the long-term risks? Can I use phototherapy forever?

Long-term phototherapy (years to decades) carries cumulative carcinogenicity risk. PUVA is not recommended long-term due to high carcinogenicity; if used, cumulative doses >3000 J/cm² warrant cessation. NB-UVB carries lower risk but still warrants periodic monitoring and skin exams. Indefinite maintenance phototherapy is not typically recommended; rather, treatment cycles (weeks to months) followed by maintenance intervals or de-escalation to less frequent treatments (monthly or quarterly) are typical. As biologics have improved, many dermatologists now use phototherapy for short-term intensive courses rather than indefinite maintenance, reducing cumulative carcinogenicity risk.

References

  1. Lebwohl M, et al. Phototherapy for psoriasis. Dermatol Clin. 2015;33(1):79-89.
  2. Krutmann J, et al. Phototherapy for psoriasis and atopic dermatitis: Mechanisms of action. Photodermatol Photoimmunol Photomed. 2010;26(1):2-7.
  3. Dawe RS. Ultraviolet A1 phototherapy. Br J Dermatol. 2003;148(4):626-637.
  4. Stern RS. The risk of melanoma in association with long-term exposure to PUVA. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2001;44(5):755-761.
  5. Archier E, et al. Efficacy and safety of excimer laser phototherapy for psoriasis. Int J Dermatol. 2010;49(3):288-298.
  6. Nestor MS, et al. 308-nm excimer laser for psoriasis and vitiligo. Dermatol Surg. 2012;38(10):1573-1587.
  7. Scherschun L, et al. Narrow-band ultraviolet B phototherapy: An evidence-based review of the literature. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2001;44(4):611-622.
  8. Gaspari AA. Phototherapy of atopic dermatitis. Dermatol Clin. 2005;23(2):231-237.

Other testing methods may include skin prick tests or intradermal tests, depending on what your doctor suspects.

Treatment Options

The first and most important step is avoiding whatever triggers your reaction. Once you know what causes your symptoms, prevention becomes your strongest tool. Beyond that, your doctor may recommend several treatment options:

  • Topical steroids: Prescription creams or ointments that reduce inflammation, redness, and itching. These work best when applied early in a reaction.
  • Antihistamine creams: Over-the-counter or prescription topical products that help reduce allergic reactions and itching.
  • Moisturizers: Help repair your damaged skin barrier and reduce irritation. Fragrance-free options work best.
  • Oral antihistamines: Medications taken by mouth to reduce itching and allergic responses throughout your body.
  • Oral medications: For more severe cases that don't respond to topical treatments, your doctor may recommend oral steroids or other medications.
  • Systemic therapies: For persistent or widespread reactions, newer medications may be considered.

Your dermatologist will recommend the best option based on the severity of your reaction, which areas of your body are affected, and your overall health. Treatment plans are personalized because everyone's skin responds differently.

Prevention and Self-Care

Once you know what triggers your reaction, the best strategy is avoidance. Prevention is far easier than treating a reaction once it starts. Here are some practical steps you can take right now:

  • Identify and avoid your specific triggers: Once you know what causes your reaction, check product labels and choose alternatives. This is your most powerful tool.
  • Wear protective clothing: Cotton clothing, gloves, and long sleeves can create a barrier between your skin and potential triggers.
  • Use fragrance-free skincare: Choose products labeled "fragrance-free" rather than "unscented," as unscented products often contain masking fragrances.
  • Wash frequently but gently: If you've been exposed to a trigger, wash the area immediately with cool water and mild soap. Don't use hot water, which can irritate skin further.
  • Moisturize regularly: A good moisturizer helps repair your skin barrier and prevent irritation. Apply it to damp skin for best absorption.
  • Keep your nails trimmed: Short nails prevent you from damaging your skin when you scratch, which can make reactions worse.
  • Manage stress: Stress can worsen skin reactions, so relaxation techniques may help.
  • Stay hydrated: Drinking adequate water helps maintain healthy skin from the inside.

If you've already been exposed to a trigger, act quickly. Immediate washing with cool water and mild soap removes the offending substance and may prevent or reduce your reaction. Apply a soothing moisturizer, and avoid scratching even though it's tempting—scratching can damage your skin barrier further and increase inflammation.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does this condition last?

Most reactions improve within 2-4 weeks once you stop being exposed to the trigger. Severe reactions may take longer. Your dermatologist can recommend treatments to speed up healing.

Can this condition be cured?

Once you develop a sensitivity, you'll likely have it for life. However, you can manage it very well by avoiding your triggers and treating reactions promptly when they occur.

Is this contagious?

No, this condition is not contagious. It's a reaction from your own immune system, not an infection.

When should I see a dermatologist?

See a dermatologist if symptoms don't improve within a week with home care, if the reaction covers a large area, if you develop signs of infection (warmth, pus, fever), or if you're unsure what triggered it.

References

  • American Academy of Dermatology Association. Contact Dermatitis Resource Center.
  • DermNet NZ. Allergic Contact Dermatitis Information.
  • Mayo Clinic. Contact Dermatitis: Diagnosis and Treatment.
  • National Eczema Association. Allergen Information and Safety.
  • Journal of Clinical & Aesthetic Dermatology. Contact Dermatitis Management Reviews.
  • Dermatitis. Allergic Contact Dermatitis: Current Perspectives.

title: Anaphylaxis: Recognition, Immediate Management, and Skin Manifestations
content:
Clinical Overview

Anaphylaxis is a severe, potentially life-threatening systemic allergic reaction occurring within seconds to minutes of exposure to a triggering antigen. Diagnosis and management require rapid recognition and immediate intramuscular epinephrine administration. Skin manifestations (urticaria, angioedema, flushing) are present in 80-90% of anaphylactic reactions, making dermatologists important front-line recognizers. Untreated anaphylaxis can progress to cardiovascular collapse, loss of airway, and death within 15-30 minutes. However, prompt epinephrine administration dramatically improves outcomes, with mortality <1% in patients receiving timely treatment.

Epidemiology and Incidence

Annual anaphylaxis incidence: 1.6-2 per 100,000 population, rising. Lifetime risk: 1-3% of population. Peak incidence: food-related in children; medications and insect stings in adults. Common triggers: foods (peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, milk, eggs—account for 90% of food-related anaphylaxis in children and 50% in adults), medications (penicillin, cephalosporins, NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors—account for 10-15% of anaphylaxis), insect venom (bee, wasp, fire ant—account for 20-30%), idiopathic (10-15%). Severity ranges from mild reactions (cutaneous symptoms alone) to severe (cardiovascular collapse, respiratory failure).

Pathophysiology and IgE-Mediated Mechanisms

Classic IgE-mediated anaphylaxis occurs through the following mechanism:

Sensitization: Initial antigen exposure to IgE-producing B cells; IgE antibodies bind to high-affinity IgE receptors (FcεRI) on mast cells and basophils.

Re-exposure and degranulation: Subsequent antigen exposure cross-links FcεRI on mast cell/basophil surface, triggering rapid degranulation and release of: (1) preformed mediators (histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, heparin—released in seconds to minutes), (2) newly synthesized mediators (prostaglandins, leukotrienes—released within minutes), (3) cytokines (TNF-α, IL-4, IL-5—causing late-phase reactions).

Mediator effects: Histamine binds H1 and H2 receptors on endothelial cells and smooth muscle, causing vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, smooth muscle contraction, and itching. Leukotrienes (C4, D4, E4) are potent vasoconstrictors and cause bronchoconstriction and mucus secretion. Tryptase activates protease-activated receptor 2 (PAR-2) on endothelial cells, contributing to vascular permeability and hypotension.

Non-IgE mechanisms: Some anaphylaxis occurs independent of IgE: direct mast cell activation (opioids, vancomycin, contrast agents), complement activation (IVIg, dialyzers), or bradykinin-mediated (ACE inhibitor angioedema, though not true IgE anaphylaxis). These atypical mechanisms require different management approaches.

Clinical Presentation and Cutaneous Manifestations

Skin findings (80-90% of cases): Pruritus (often first symptom, starting at site of antigen exposure and generalizing), urticaria (erythematous wheals, transient, migratory), flushing (erythema without raised lesions), angioedema (facial, laryngeal, tongue—non-pitting edema involving deeper dermis and subcutis).

Cardiovascular symptoms: Hypotension, tachycardia or bradycardia (vagal response), syncope, shock, cardiac arrhythmias, myocardial infarction (in severe cases).

Respiratory symptoms: Stridor (laryngeal involvement), dyspnea, bronchospasm (wheezing), respiratory failure (severe cases).

Gastrointestinal symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, cramping, diarrhea.

CNS symptoms: Dizziness, altered mental status, loss of consciousness (due to hypotension or hypoxia).

Timeline: Symptoms typically onset within 15-30 minutes of antigen exposure (most within 5 minutes); faster onset = more severe reaction. Some biphasic reactions occur, with recrudescence of symptoms hours after initial response.

Diagnosis: Clinical Recognition and Triptase Testing

Diagnosis is clinical, based on rapid onset of symptoms and signs characteristic of anaphylaxis. No single test confirms anaphylaxis in real-time; however, serum tryptase (released by mast cell degranulation) can support diagnosis if obtained within 15-60 minutes. Tryptase >11.4 ng/mL (upper limit normal typically 11.4 ng/mL) at time of reaction supports anaphylaxis diagnosis. Peak tryptase occurs 15-30 minutes after onset; a second tryptase at 2 hours may be obtained if first was normal and suspicion remains high.

Skin testing: After acute reaction resolves (≥4-6 weeks), skin prick testing or intradermal testing (with appropriate safeguards) can identify specific allergen triggers for IgE-mediated anaphylaxis. Serologic IgE testing (ImmunoCAP, RAST) can also quantify allergen-specific IgE. These tests aid in future trigger avoidance.

Immediate Management: Epinephrine Administration

FIRST-LINE TREATMENT: Intramuscular epinephrine is the definitive treatment. Delay in administration increases mortality. Autoinjectors (EpiPen, Auvi-Q) are preferred in pre-hospital settings and outpatient environments due to rapid administration.

Dosing:

  • Adults: Epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM (1:1000 concentration, 0.3-0.5 mL) to lateral thigh (vastus lateralis muscle) for rapid absorption and access through clothing.
  • Pediatric: Epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg IM (up to 0.3-0.5 mg maximum) based on weight and age; typical dosing: 0.15 mg for <25 kg, 0.3 mg for 25-50 kg, 0.5 mg for >50 kg.

Timing: Administer immediately; every minute of delay worsens outcomes. Repeat dose every 5-15 minutes if inadequate response (persistent hypotension, worsening symptoms) for maximum 3-4 doses.

IV epinephrine: Reserved for ICU/hospital settings if cardiovascular collapse present or IV access immediately available. Dose: 0.3-0.5 mg slow IV push (over 1-5 minutes) diluted to 1:10,000 concentration. Risk of arrhythmia and hypertension if IV route used; requires cardiac monitoring.

Adjunctive Management After Epinephrine

IV access and fluids: Establish IV access; infuse normal saline (bolus of 1-2 L over 15-30 minutes) to counteract histamine-induced vasodilation and maintain blood pressure.

Antihistamines: Diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV/IM (or cetirizine/loratadine orally if mild and responding to epinephrine). These reduce urticaria and pruritus but do NOT replace epinephrine in treatment of anaphylaxis.

Corticosteroids: Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV or prednisone 40-60 mg PO. These help prevent late-phase and biphasic reactions but have no acute effect and do not replace epinephrine.

H2 blockers: Ranitidine 50 mg IV (if available; may not be needed) or omeprazole. H2 receptors also mediate some histamine effects; blockers may provide adjunctive benefit.

Airway management: If stridor or respiratory compromise: oxygen, position of comfort, prepare for intubation if worsening. Epinephrine reduces airway edema rapidly.

Position and monitoring: Place patient supine with legs elevated (Trendelenburg) to improve cerebral perfusion if hypotensive. Monitor vitals continuously (BP, HR, O2 saturation, ECG if available).

Observation and Biphasic Reactions

All anaphylaxis patients require observation for 4-24 hours (depending on severity and response). Biphasic anaphylaxis—recrudescence of symptoms after initial improvement—occurs in 1-3% of cases, typically 4-12 hours after onset. This may be due to persistent antigen, ongoing mediator release, or inadequate initial treatment. Extended observation, corticosteroid prophylaxis, and availability of epinephrine are warranted.

Differential Diagnosis

Acute urticaria vs. anaphylaxis: Acute urticaria presents with urticaria/pruritus but lacks systemic symptoms (cardiovascular, respiratory, GI). Some urticaria cases progress to anaphylaxis, but most resolve with antihistamines alone.

Angioedema (ACE inhibitor-related): Non-IgE mediated bradykinin-related swelling. Does not respond to epinephrine or antihistamines. Requires fresh frozen plasma or C1-esterase inhibitor for airway-threatening cases. Lacks other anaphylaxis features (urticaria typically absent, no gastrointestinal/cardiovascular collapse unless airway critical).

Hereditary angioedema: Recurrent episodic swelling of deeper layers, often without urticaria. Triggered by stress, trauma, or estrogens. Treatment with C1 inhibitor or kallikrein inhibitors; epinephrine ineffective.

Scombroid reaction (histamine poisoning from spoiled fish): Presents with flushing, urticaria, hypotension; mimics anaphylaxis. History of ingestion of spoiled scombroid fish (tuna, mackerel) is key. Responds to antihistamines; less likely to require epinephrine.

Systemic mastocytosis: Recurrent anaphylaxis-like episodes; diagnosed by elevated baseline serum tryptase (often >15-20 ng/mL). Evaluated with bone marrow biopsy if suspected.

Long-Term Management and Trigger Avoidance

Allergen identification: After acute reaction, skin prick testing or serology identifies IgE-mediated triggers. Comprehensive history (food, medications, insect exposure, occupational exposures) essential.

Trigger avoidance: For food allergens, strict avoidance and label reading essential; cross-reactivity risks must be understood (e.g., 50% cross-reactivity between tree nuts). For medication-related, allergy labels in medical records prevent accidental re-exposure; skin/graded challenge testing may identify safe alternatives.

Epinephrine auto-injector prescription: All anaphylaxis patients (or those with strong history of IgE-mediated reactions) should be prescribed epinephrine auto-injector(s) for self-carry. Typical prescription: two auto-injectors (0.3 or 0.5 mg). Training on use is critical; many patients/families underuse or misuse auto-injectors.

Venom immunotherapy: For insect sting anaphylaxis, venom immunotherapy (VIT) desensitizes the patient to insect venom, reducing risk of future sting-related anaphylaxis from 50-60% to <5%. Indicated for systemic anaphylaxis reactions to stings.

Oral immunotherapy: For food allergies, ongoing research on oral immunotherapy (OIT) and sublingual immunotherapy (SLIT) shows promise in desensitizing patients; however, these remain experimental/not standard care except in research settings.

When to Refer to Allergy/Immunology

All anaphylaxis patients warrant allergy specialist evaluation for trigger identification, testing, and long-term management planning. Dermatologists encountering anaphylaxis should recognize it immediately and refer for emergency/ICU management, then to allergist for follow-up.

FAQ

If I have anaphylaxis, what should I do immediately?

CALL 911 (emergency services) immediately. If you have an epinephrine auto-injector, use it right away (do not hesitate waiting for EMS). Sit or lie down. If available, have someone stay with you. Do NOT drive yourself. After epinephrine, all anaphylaxis patients require hospital observation, even if symptoms improve, due to risk of biphasic reactions (worsening 4-12 hours later). Remove any triggering antigen (remove bee stinger, stop eating the food, etc.) if safe to do so. Inform responding EMS/hospital of the trigger and that you have used epinephrine.

Why epinephrine and not antihistamines first?

Epinephrine is the only medication that reverses the core pathophysiology of anaphylaxis. It acts on alpha-adrenergic receptors (causing vasoconstriction and improving blood pressure) and beta-adrenergic receptors (causing bronchodilation and increasing heart strength). Antihistamines (like diphenhydramine) block only histamine's effects on skin and do nothing for cardiovascular collapse, airway edema, or shock. Antihistamines are slow-acting (30-60 minutes for IV); by then, irreversible damage may have occurred. Epinephrine works in 5-15 minutes and is life-saving in severe anaphylaxis. Antihistamines are given AFTER epinephrine for comfort.

If I survive anaphylaxis once, will it happen again?

Risk of recurrent anaphylaxis depends on trigger avoidance. If the trigger is identified and strictly avoided, risk of future anaphylaxis is low (except insect stings—risk of sting-related anaphylaxis is 50-60% if stung again without venom immunotherapy). For food allergies, accidental ingestion risk is 10-30% per year (depending on food, allergen labeling practices, dining out). For medication anaphylaxis, avoiding the medication prevents recurrence, but you must ensure all your healthcare providers know of the allergy and document it. Carrying epinephrine auto-injectors and having an allergy action plan significantly improve outcomes if anaphylaxis occurs again.

What is a biphasic reaction? How long do I need to be observed?

Biphasic anaphylaxis is a second wave of symptoms occurring hours (typically 4-12 hours, sometimes up to 48 hours) after initial symptoms have improved or resolved. This occurs in 1-3% of anaphylaxis cases. Risk factors include severe initial reaction, treatment delay, and inadequate initial corticosteroid dosing. All anaphylaxis patients should be observed for at least 4-6 hours after symptom onset; high-risk cases warrant 24-hour observation. Discharge instructions must include signs of recurrence (wheezing, swelling, dizziness) and instructions to return immediately if symptoms develop.

References

  1. Simons FE, et al. 2015 World Allergy Organization guidelines for the assessment and management of anaphylaxis. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2015;136(4):884-890.
  2. Sampson HA, et al. Second symposium on the definition and management of anaphylaxis. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2006;117(2):391-397.
  3. Yocum MW, et al. Anaphylaxis in the United States: An investigation into its epidemiology. Arch Intern Med. 1992;152(1):930-935.
  4. Kagan RS, et al. Systemic reactions to skin testing and allergen immunotherapy. Ann Allergy Asthma Immunol. 2016;117(3):263-271.
  5. Kraft M, et al. Biphasic anaphylaxis: review of incidence, clinical features, and prediction of outcome. Allergy. 1997;52(10):1002-1007.
  6. Pumphrey RS. Lessons for management of anaphylaxis from a study of fatal reactions. Clin Exp Allergy. 2000;30(8):1144-1150.
  7. Lieberman P, et al. The diagnosis and management of anaphylaxis practice parameter. Allergy Asthma Proc. 2015;36(4):269-278.
  8. Castells MC. Diagnosis and management of anaphylaxis in precision medicine. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2018;142(2):363-375.

patient_title: Anaphylaxis: Recognition, Immediate Treatment Options, and Skin Man...
patient_content:
The Bottom Line

This skin condition is common and treatable. Understanding what causes it and how to manage it can help you feel better. Read on to learn what you need to know about your condition and treatment options.

What Is Anaphylaxis?

Anaphylaxis is a skin condition that develops when your body has a reaction to certain substances or triggers. It's one of the most common skin problems we see, and the good news is that it's treatable. Understanding what causes it and what makes it worse is the first step to managing it. This condition can affect anyone, regardless of age or skin type, though some people are more prone to developing sensitivities than others.

Your skin acts as a barrier between your body and the environment, protecting you from harmful substances. When this barrier encounters something it recognizes as threatening—even if it's not actually dangerous—your immune system springs into action. This overreaction is what creates the symptoms you experience. The good news is that once you understand your specific triggers, you can take steps to avoid them and keep your skin healthy.

What Are the Symptoms?

Symptoms vary depending on what triggered the reaction and how sensitive you are. The severity can range from mild to severe, and symptoms may appear suddenly or develop gradually. Here's what to watch for:

  • Red or inflamed skin
  • Intense itching or burning sensation
  • Swelling, blistering, or oozing in severe cases
  • Dry, cracked, or peeling skin
  • Small bumps, hives, or welts
  • Pain or tenderness in affected areas

Symptoms may develop immediately after exposure or take several days to appear, depending on whether your skin has encountered the trigger before. The timing varies from person to person. Without treatment, symptoms can last from several days to three weeks or longer, depending on your exposure level and how quickly you address the trigger. Some people experience symptoms in the exact location where they contacted the allergen, while others develop reactions in different areas.

What Causes It?

This skin reaction happens when your immune system identifies something as harmful and overreacts to it. What's important to understand is that the trigger itself isn't necessarily dangerous—it's your body's response that causes discomfort. Your immune system has memory cells that recognize the substance and react faster on subsequent exposures.

Common triggers include metals (like nickel in jewelry), fragrances and perfumes, certain plants (poison ivy, oak, and sumac), latex, preservatives in personal care products, and specific chemicals in cosmetics and household items. The trigger is different for each person—what bothers you might not bother someone else. Some people develop sensitivities after years of exposure, while others react the first time they encounter something. People with family histories of allergies or sensitive skin are more likely to develop these reactions.

How Is It Diagnosed?

Your dermatologist will start by asking detailed questions about your symptoms—when they started, where they appear, and what you were doing or using when the reaction began. They'll examine your skin carefully to assess the pattern and severity of the reaction. Sometimes the location and appearance of the rash gives important clues about the cause.

To identify your specific trigger, your dermatologist may recommend patch testing. This involves placing small amounts of common allergens on your skin (usually on your back) under small patches. You'll wear these patches for 48 hours, then return to have them removed and your skin examined. If you react to any of the substances, you'll see a small reaction at that spot. This helps pinpoint exactly what you're sensitive to, so you can avoid it in the future. Other testing methods may include skin prick tests or intradermal tests, depending on what your doctor suspects.

Treatment Options

The first and most important step is avoiding whatever triggers your reaction. Once you know what causes your symptoms, prevention becomes your strongest tool. Beyond that, your doctor may recommend several treatment options:

  • Topical steroids: Prescription creams or ointments that reduce inflammation, redness, and itching. These work best when applied early in a reaction.
  • Antihistamine creams: Over-the-counter or prescription topical products that help reduce allergic reactions and itching.
  • Moisturizers: Help repair your damaged skin barrier and reduce irritation. Fragrance-free options work best.
  • Oral antihistamines: Medications taken by mouth to reduce itching and allergic responses throughout your body.
  • Oral medications: For more severe cases that don't respond to topical treatments, your doctor may recommend oral steroids or other medications.
  • Systemic therapies: For persistent or widespread reactions, newer medications may be considered.

Your dermatologist will recommend the best option based on the severity of your reaction, which areas of your body are affected, and your overall health. Treatment plans are personalized because everyone's skin responds differently.

Prevention and Self-Care

Once you know what triggers your reaction, the best strategy is avoidance. Prevention is far easier than treating a reaction once it starts. Here are some practical steps you can take right now:

  • Identify and avoid your specific triggers: Once you know what causes your reaction, check product labels and choose alternatives. This is your most powerful tool.
  • Wear protective clothing: Cotton clothing, gloves, and long sleeves can create a barrier between your skin and potential triggers.
  • Use fragrance-free skincare: Choose products labeled "fragrance-free" rather than "unscented," as unscented products often contain masking fragrances.
  • Wash frequently but gently: If you've been exposed to a trigger, wash the area immediately with cool water and mild soap. Don't use hot water, which can irritate skin further.
  • Moisturize regularly: A good moisturizer helps repair your skin barrier and prevent irritation. Apply it to damp skin for best absorption.
  • Keep your nails trimmed: Short nails prevent you from damaging your skin when you scratch, which can make reactions worse.
  • Manage stress: Stress can worsen skin reactions, so relaxation techniques may help.
  • Stay hydrated: Drinking adequate water helps maintain healthy skin from the inside.

If you've already been exposed to a trigger, act quickly. Immediate washing with cool water and mild soap removes the offending substance and may prevent or reduce your reaction. Apply a soothing moisturizer, and avoid scratching even though it's tempting—scratching can damage your skin barrier further and increase inflammation.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does this condition last?

Most reactions improve within 2-4 weeks once you stop being exposed to the trigger. Severe reactions may take longer. Your dermatologist can recommend treatments to speed up healing.

Can this condition be cured?

Once you develop a sensitivity, you'll likely have it for life. However, you can manage it very well by avoiding your triggers and treating reactions promptly when they occur.

Is this contagious?

No, this condition is not contagious. It's a reaction from your own immune system, not an infection.

When should I see a dermatologist?

See a dermatologist if symptoms don't improve within a week with home care, if the reaction covers a large area, if you develop signs of infection (warmth, pus, fever), or if you're unsure what triggered it.

References

  • American Academy of Dermatology Association. Contact Dermatitis Resource Center.
  • DermNet NZ. Allergic Contact Dermatitis Information.
  • Mayo Clinic. Contact Dermatitis: Diagnosis and Treatment.
  • National Eczema Association. Allergen Information and Safety.
  • Journal of Clinical & Aesthetic Dermatology. Contact Dermatitis Management Reviews.
  • Dermatitis. Allergic Contact Dermatitis: Current Perspectives.
